Handlers Effects & replay
More
rewindjs.com
Reference

Handlers

A handler module is one TEA update function with its case-arms hoisted to named exports. The runtime dispatches each activation to the matching export; the body accumulates effects and the return value picks the connection's disposition.

Activation kinds

The runtime has a fixed set of activation kinds (the Msg sum). A module exports the subset it cares about; the runtime introspects exports at load and routes each activation to its export.

ExportFires on
defaultinbound HTTP — the 80% case
onResultan outbound on.fetch completed (its response, as a recorded message)
onTimeran on.timer(ms) elapsed
onKva watched on.kv(prefix) key changed
onMessage / onCloseinbound connection frames / disconnect

Each export is a separate, deterministic activation with a fresh arena. They share nothing but kv (the Model) and any ctx you thread forward with next().

The request surface

request is an ambient global describing the current activation. Reading it is recorded — the values are taped, so a replay sees exactly what the live request saw.

FieldIs
request.methodHTTP method
request.pathrequest path
request.queryraw query string
request.headerslower-cased header map
request.bodythe buffered body (≤ 1 MB; larger bodies stream — see below)
request.resulton a callback activation: the recorded message that resumed you

The response head

Status, headers, and cookies are the ambient response global — not return arguments. The head is sent on the first output or the terminal return.

response.status  = 201;
response.headers = { "content-type": "text/event-stream" };
response.cookies = ["sid=…; HttpOnly"];

The five roles

Everything a handler does sits on one axis: does it act on the current connection (the held socket — ephemeral, dies when the caller disconnects) or create a connectionless request (a fresh activation, no socket — durable)? The verb is the scope. There is no durable: flag — you choose by which verb you reach for.

RoleSurfaceScope
Modelkv.get / kv.set / kv.delete / kv.prefixread-your-writes
Connection outputstream.start() / stream.write(chunk)current connection
Connection triggerson.timer / on.kv / on.fetchcurrent connection (ephemeral)
Connectionless triggerswebhook.send / schedule / cronconnectionless (durable)
Dispositionnext({ctx?}) · terminal bodythe return value
The litmus test for which scope: if the caller closed their laptop right now, should this still happen? No → a current- connection verb. Yes → a connectionless verb.

Disposition — the return value

The return is exactly one of two things:

ReturnMeans
string / any value → JSONterminal response: commit, ship body + head, close
next({ctx?})held: commit, hold the connection, resume when a registered trigger fires

There is no stream return verb. You stream by calling stream.write() and returning next(); you close by returning a terminal body. next() alone commits nothing — the head stays open, so a later resume can still set the status and body (the await-then-respond / gateway pattern).

Worked examples

Buffered, with auth and status

export default function () {
  if (!authorized(request.headers)) { response.status = 401; return "unauthorized"; }
  return process(request.body);
}

Gateway — hold the client, forward upstream, return its status

export default function () {
  on.fetch("https://upstream/api", { method: "POST", body: request.body });
  return next();                          // hold, uncommitted
}
export function onResult() {
  response.status = request.result.status;     // forward upstream's status
  return request.result.body;
}

Fire durable work, respond now

export default function () {
  webhook.send({ url: "https://hook/notify", body: request.body });
  return "accepted";   // the webhook outlives this request
}
